content.text: are saved snapshots of a model's entire internal state at specific points during training. They include the model's
, and other variables necessary to resume training or deploy the model later.
in a video game — they let you resume training from exactly where you stopped instead of starting from scratch.
Why Checkpoints Matter in Generative AI
Training generative models (GANs, diffusion models, LLMs) takes
Resume from last checkpoint instead of restarting
Recover progress without losing weeks of training
Use "early stopping" — pick best checkpoint before overfitting
Save periodically to balance recovery flexibility vs disk space
Use checkpoint as starting point for new task/dataset
Resume training on different clusters from checkpoint
that prevents loss of progress due to interruptions like hardware failures, software crashes, or manual stops.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

# Model, optimizer, loss
model = nn.Linear(128, 784)
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
criterion = nn.MSELoss()

# Training loop
for epoch in range(100):
    for inputs, labels in dataset:
        # Forward pass
        outputs = model(inputs)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        
        # Backward pass
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
    
    # SAVE CHECKPOINT at end of each epoch
    checkpoint = {
        'epoch': epoch,
        'model_state': model.state_dict(),  # All weights [web:119]
        'optimizer_state': optimizer.state_dict(),  # Optimizer state [web:119]
        'loss': loss.item()
    }
    
    torch.save(checkpoint, f'checkpoint_epoch_{epoch}.pth')  # Save to disk [web:119]

# Load checkpoint
checkpoint = torch.load('checkpoint_epoch_50.pth')

# Restore model state
model.load_state_dict(checkpoint['model_state'])  # [web:119]

# Restore optimizer state
optimizer.load_state_dict(checkpoint['optimizer_state'])  # [web:119]

# Resume training
for epoch in range(checkpoint['epoch'], 100):  # Start from epoch 50
    for inputs, labels in dataset:
        outputs = model(inputs)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
        
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

import tensorflow as tf

# Model
model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(256, activation='relu', input_shape=(128,)),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(784, activation='sigmoid')
])

# Loss and optimizer
loss_fn = tf.keras.losses.MeanSquaredError()
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=0.001)

# ModelCheckpoint callback (saves automatically)
checkpoint_callback = tf.keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint(
    filepath='checkpoints/model_epoch_{epoch}',  # Save path [web:121]
    save_weights_only=True,  # Save weights only [web:115]
    save_best_only=False,    # Save every epoch [web:115]
    verbose=1
)

# Training with automatic checkpointing
model.fit(
    train_ds,
    epochs=100,
    callbacks=[checkpoint_callback]  # Automatic saving [web:121]
)

# Save only the best checkpoint (lowest validation loss)
checkpoint_callback = tf.keras.callbacks.ModelCheckpoint(
    filepath='checkpoints/best_model',
    save_weights_only=True,
    save_best_only=True,  # Only save if val_loss improves [web:121]
    monitor='val_loss',   # Monitor validation loss [web:121]
    verbose=1
)

model.fit(
    train_ds,
    epochs=100,
    validation_data=val_ds,
    callbacks=[checkpoint_callback]
)

# Training loop with manual checkpointing
for epoch in range(100):
    with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
        predictions = model(train_ds, training=True)
        loss = loss_fn(train_labels, predictions)
    
    gradients = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
    optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(gradients, model.trainable_variables))
    
    # SAVE CHECKPOINT manually
    if epoch % 10 == 0:  # Every 10 epochs
        model.save_weights(f'checkpoints/epoch_{epoch}')  # Save weights [web:121]
        print(f'Saved checkpoint at epoch {epoch}')
    
    # LOAD CHECKPOINT to resume
    # model.load_weights('checkpoints/epoch_50')
High disk usage (terabytes for large models)

# Strategy 1: Regular checkpoints + best checkpoint
if epoch % 10 == 0:
    torch.save(checkpoint, f'checkpoint_epoch_{epoch}.pth')  # Regular [web:118]

if loss < best_loss:
    torch.save(checkpoint, 'checkpoint_best.pth')  # Best [web:121]

# Strategy 2: Incremental checkpoints (overwrite same file)
torch.save(checkpoint, 'checkpoint_latest.pth')  # Always overwrite [web:118]

# Strategy 3: Checkpoint + validation
torch.save(checkpoint, f'checkpoint_epoch_{epoch}.pth')
evaluate_model(model)  # Check if validation loss improved

# Save both generator and discriminator
checkpoint = {
    'epoch': epoch,
    'generator_state': generator.state_dict(),
    'discriminator_state': discriminator.state_dict(),
    'g_optimizer_state': g_optimizer.state_dict(),
    'd_optimizer_state': d_optimizer.state_dict(),
    'g_loss': g_loss.item(),
    'd_loss': d_loss.item()
}

torch.save(checkpoint, f'gan_checkpoint_epoch_{epoch}.pth')

# Diffusion models are large (hundreds of MB to GB)
checkpoint = {
    'epoch': epoch,
    'model_state': diffusion_model.state_dict(),
    'optimizer_state': optimizer.state_dict(),
    'timestep': epoch * batch_size,
    'loss': loss.item()
}

# Save to cloud storage (等大型 models)
torch.save(checkpoint, f's3://checkpoints/diffusion_epoch_{epoch}.pth')

# LLMs can be terabytes in size
checkpoint = {
    'epoch': epoch,
    'model_state': model.state_dict(),
    'optimizer_state': optimizer.state_dict(),
    'learning_rate': optimizer.lr,
    'total_tokens': epoch * batch_size * sequence_length
}

# Use distributed saving (multiple files)
from torch.distributed import checkpoint
checkpoint.save(checkpoint, 'llm_checkpoints/epoch_{epoch}')

# WRONG: Only save model state
torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'checkpoint.pth')  # Can't resume training [web:120]

# RIGHT: Save model + optimizer state
checkpoint = {
    'model_state': model.state_dict(),
    'optimizer_state': optimizer.state_dict()  # Required for Adam/momentum [web:120]
}
torch.save(checkpoint, 'checkpoint.pth')

checkpoint = {
    'model_state': model.state_dict(),
    'optimizer_state': optimizer.state_dict(),
    'epoch': epoch,
    'loss': loss.item(),
    'val_loss': val_loss,  # For early stopping [web:121]
    'best_loss': best_loss
}

# Overwrite same file to save disk space
torch.save(checkpoint, 'checkpoint_latest.pth')  # Always latest [web:118]

# Save checkpoint before expensive validation
torch.save(checkpoint, f'checkpoint_epoch_{epoch}.pth')

# Run validation
val_loss = evaluate_model(model)  # May take hours
: Keep reading from where you left (resume training)
of the AI model that enable training to resume from a specific point in case of interruptions.
A GPU crash, power failure, or cloud preemption during a 72-hour training run does not lose all progress — the model resumes from the last saved checkpoint.
By tracking validation loss across checkpoints, you can select the epoch where generalization was best, rather than using the final (potentially overfitted) model.
A checkpoint from pre-training on one dataset becomes the starting point for fine-tuning on a different task — avoiding the cost of training from scratch.
Saving the optimizer state captures the accumulated momentum and adaptive learning rate history, so resuming training is mathematically equivalent to uninterrupted training.
Multiple fine-tuning experiments can branch from the same checkpoint, making it easy to compare different hyperparameter choices without re-running expensive pre-training.
Training can be paused on an expensive on-demand instance, checkpoint saved to object storage (S3/GCS), and resumed later on a spot instance or a different cluster.
Checkpoints from different training stages can be kept and served as separate model versions — reverting to an earlier model when a new one regresses is a matter of loading an older checkpoint.
Checkpointing is not free — it has real costs that must be weighed against the recovery benefits:

Saving a checkpoint serializes all tensors to disk synchronously by default. During this time, all GPU computation is halted. For a 7B parameter model (28 GB in FP32), a checkpoint save can pause training for 30–120 seconds.

Each checkpoint for a large model can consume tens to hundreds of gigabytes. Storing 10 checkpoints for a 70B model (280 GB each in FP32) requires ~2.8 TB — plus optimizer state, which can double the size.
A crash during the checkpoint save itself produces a corrupt file. Writing to a temp file and atomically renaming it mitigates this, but adds complexity.
serialization, which can execute arbitrary code when loaded. Loading a checkpoint from an untrusted source is a remote code execution vulnerability.
In multi-GPU or multi-node training, each rank has part of the model. Coordinating checkpoints across ranks while preventing deadlocks requires additional infrastructure (e.g., PyTorch's
A checkpoint saved with PyTorch 2.1 may not load cleanly under PyTorch 2.3 if internal tensor formats or optimizer state keys have changed between versions.

A checkpoint integrates into the training loop at the epoch or step boundary. The core flow is: complete a training unit → evaluate on validation data → decide whether to save → serialize state to disk → continue (or resume from saved state if restarting after failure).

(momentum buffers, adaptive learning rate squares, step counts), the
(so training knows where to resume), and
(so early-stopping logic can continue correctly). Omitting the optimizer state means the model can be deployed but not trained further from that checkpoint.
Checkpoint size scales linearly with model parameter count. For common configurations:

blocks training while writing to disk. Async checkpointing decouples the GPU computation from the I/O by first copying state to CPU RAM (fast), then writing to disk in a background thread (slow, but doesn't block training):

import threading, copy, torch

def async_checkpoint(state_dict, path):
    """Copy model state to CPU, then write to disk in a background thread."""
    # Detach all tensors and move to CPU — fast (GPU to CPU memcopy)
    cpu_state = {k: v.cpu() for k, v in state_dict.items()}

    def write():
        torch.save(cpu_state, path)

    t = threading.Thread(target=write, daemon=True)
    t.start()
    return t   # caller can join() to ensure write finishes before exit

# Usage in training loop
for epoch in range(n_epochs):
    train_one_epoch(model, optimizer, loader)
    if epoch % 10 == 0:
        # Checkpoint write happens in background — GPU continues training
        writer = async_checkpoint(model.state_dict(), f'ckpt_{epoch}.pth')

writer.join()  # ensure last checkpoint is written before script exits
with built-in async support for distributed training, eliminating the need for manual threading.

import os, glob

def save_checkpoint_with_rotation(state, path_template, max_keep=3):
    """Save checkpoint and delete oldest if over limit."""
    # Atomic write: write to temp, rename
    tmp = path_template + '.tmp'
    torch.save(state, tmp)
    os.rename(tmp, path_template)   # atomic on POSIX filesystems

    # Rotate: keep only max_keep most recent checkpoints
    checkpoints = sorted(glob.glob(path_template.replace('{epoch}', '*')))
    for old in checkpoints[:-max_keep]:
        os.remove(old)
        print(f'Removed old checkpoint: {old}')
Unsafe — can execute code on load
A training checkpoint differs from a model export (ONNX, TorchScript, SavedModel). A checkpoint is designed for
— it includes optimizer state and metadata. A model export is designed for
— it includes weights and the computational graph, optimized for deployment, but not for resuming training.
module, which can execute arbitrary Python code when deserializing objects. Loading a

file from an untrusted source — a public download, a user-uploaded file, a CI artifact from an unverified pull request — is a remote code execution vulnerability. The safetensors format from Hugging Face solves this by using a flat binary format with no code execution capability:

# UNSAFE: arbitrary code can run during load
model.load_state_dict(torch.load('checkpoint_from_internet.pth'))

# SAFE: safetensors format — no code execution possible
from safetensors.torch import save_file, load_file

# Saving
save_file(model.state_dict(), 'model.safetensors')

# Loading — safe even from untrusted sources
state_dict = load_file('model.safetensors')
model.load_state_dict(state_dict)
A crash during checkpoint write produces a corrupt file. To prevent this, write to a temporary file and rename atomically:
For checkpoints stored in cloud object storage (S3, GCS), verify the SHA-256 hash of the file after download before loading to detect silent corruption during network transfer:

import hashlib

def verify_checkpoint(path, expected_sha256):
    """Verify checkpoint file integrity before loading."""
    sha256 = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(8192), b''):
            sha256.update(chunk)
    actual = sha256.hexdigest()
    if actual != expected_sha256:
        raise ValueError(
            f'Checkpoint integrity check failed.\n'
            f'  Expected: {expected_sha256}\n'
            f'  Got:      {actual}'
        )
Never load .pth files from untrusted sources
— use safetensors format for any checkpoint that will be shared publicly or loaded from external parties
using a temp-file-then-rename pattern to avoid corrupt checkpoints after crashes
when retrieving checkpoints from remote storage to catch file corruption during transfer
— a malicious actor who can write to your checkpoint directory can inject arbitrary code that executes the next time training resumes
= Snapshot of model's entire internal state (weights, optimizer, epoch, loss)
: Fault tolerance — resume training from last checkpoint instead of restarting
: Architecture, weights, biases, optimizer state, learning rate, epoch count
: Balance between recovery flexibility (every epoch) vs disk space (every 10 epochs)
: Save only when validation loss improves (early stopping)
: Essential for days/weeks of training (GANs, diffusion, LLMs)
: Use checkpoint as starting point for new task/dataset
: Resume training on different clusters/nodes from checkpoint
: Checkpointing halts all GPU activity during save
: Overwrite same file to save disk space

in deep learning that significantly improves efficiency, robustness, and flexibility during training, enabling you to resume training, explore different hyperparameters, recover from failures, and smoothly deploy trained models.
